title: The role of Wigner rotation in estimating the specific angular momentum of a Kerr spacetime
authors: F. J. Lobo, M. Rivera-Tapia, G. Rubilar, A. Delgado
abstract: We study the rotation of the polarization due to the gravitational field in the Kerr spacetime and the possibility of estimating the specific angular momentum that parameterizes this metric. Our approach is based on a geodesic interferometer, that is, a Mach-Zehnder interferometer whose arms are defined by null geodesics, and a single photon propagating within it. We show that the detection probability at the output ports of the interferometer is a function of two phase differences, one arising from the gravitational time delay and the other from the polarization rotation, both computed under the slow rotation and weak field approximations. Thereby, the interferometric visibility is a signature of two relativistic effects. Using the detection probability, we obtain an estimate for the specific angular momentum and characterize its uncertainty.
summary: This theoretical paper develops a method to measure the specific angular momentum of a Kerr spacetime using a quantum geodesic interferometer. By analyzing the interference visibility, which depends on both gravitational time delay and polarization rotation (Wigner phase), the authors derive an estimate for the spacetime parameter $a$. The results suggest that such measurements could achieve high precision with sufficiently large detector separations.
main_problem: The paper aims to estimate the specific angular momentum ($a$) of a Kerr spacetime by analyzing the polarization rotation of light.
main_result: The interferometric visibility is a signature of two relativistic effects (time delay and polarization rotation), allowing for an estimate of $a$ with a relative error potentially reaching $10^{-6}$ for large baselines.
method: The analysis uses a geodesic interferometer model, calculating the detection probability based on phase differences derived using the WKB approximation in the weak-field and slow-rotation limits.
model_or_system: The system is light (single photon) propagating along null geodesics within the curved background of a Kerr spacetime, analyzed using the tetrad formalism.
key_observables: Interferometric visibility, detection probability ($P_+$), gravitational time delay phase difference ($\Delta\vartheta_	au$), and Wigner phase difference ($\Delta\vartheta$).
important_parameters: Specific angular momentum ($a$), mass ($m$), interferometer separation ($\Delta r$), and probability error ($\delta p$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the slow rotation and weak field approximations, and the geometric optics approximation for the photon's localized qubit representation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geodesic interferometer setup, showing the dependence of phase differences ($\Delta\vartheta_	au$ and $\Delta\vartheta$) on source positions and angular coordinates, and the relative error $\delta a/a$ vs. baseline separation $\Delta r$.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by defining the interferometer setup, calculating the two distinct phase contributions (time delay and Wigner rotation) using the WKB approximation, and finally deriving the estimate for $a$ from the measured detection probability $P_+$.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, quantum-metrology approach to measuring fundamental parameters of General Relativity (like $a$) using quantum interference effects, bridging quantum optics and gravitational physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Geodesic interferometer: A single photon emit- ted from a source at radial coordinate r2 is prepared in a superposition of states propagating along two paths. One path component travels towards r1 and reflects, while the other travels towards r3 and reflects. Both reflected com- ponents subsequently propagate to the radial coordinate r4, where they are recombined and measured.
FIG. 2: Phase difference ∆ϑτ from Eq. (56) as a function of r2 for ω1 = 106 Hz (red line), ω2 = 1010 Hz (blue line) and ω3 = 1012 Hz (yellow line), with l = 0.3 m and h = 0.7 m.

FIG. 3: Wigner phase rotation ∆φ from Eq. (58) as a function of the angular coordinate θ. Each line represents the distance r2 of the source of photons from the center of the Earth for ∆r = r3 −r1 = 1 m. The red continuous line corresponds to r2 = REarth and the blue continuous line to r2 = 2 REarth.

FIG. 4: Wigner phase rotation ∆φ from Eq. (58) as a function of the angular coordinate θ. Each line represents the distance r2 of the source of photons from the center of the Earth for ∆r = r3 −r1 ≈2 × 103 m. The red continuous line corresponds to r2 = 1.1 REarth and the blue continuous line to r2 = 2 REarth.

Figure 3 shows ∆ϑ as a function of the angular coor- dinate θ for the case of Earth with REarth ≈7 × 106 m and ∆r := r3 −r1 ≈1 m with r2 = REarth (red line) and r2 = 2REarth (blue line). As is apparent from this figure, the Wigner phase ∆ϑ reaches values of the order of 10−30, which is several orders of magnitude smaller than the phase shift ∆ϑτ generated by the arrival time difference. The value of ∆ϑ can be increased by consid- ering higher values of ∆r. This is shown in Fig. 4, where ∆r ≈2 × 103 m, leading to Wigner phase values on the order of 10−23.
